statement: World Bank data for 2024 shows Iceland's GDP per capita at purchasing power parity (current international $) at approximately $84,300, placing it second among the Nordic countries after Norway ($102,000). Denmark follows at $81,900, Sweden at $71,800, and Finland at $65,400. All five Nordic countries substantially exceed the EU-27 average. Iceland's GDP per capita has recovered strongly from the 2008 financial crisis, when it experienced an 8.3% GDP contraction.
caveats: GDP per capita PPP figures from World Bank and Eurostat may differ slightly due to different PPP conversion methodologies and base years. Iceland's high GDP per capita partly reflects energy-intensive aluminium smelting, which adds to GDP but is largely foreign-owned. Norway's figure is heavily influenced by petroleum sector revenues.

related_entries.statement: In 2024, Iceland's GDP per capita in purchasing power standards (PPS) was approximately €52,300, compared to the EU-27 average of €39,700. Among Nordic peers, Norway led at €64,500 (boosted by petroleum wealth), Denmark stood at €50,800, Sweden at €44,700, and Finland at €40,900. Iceland thus ranks second among the Nordics and approximately 32% above the EU average, though below Norway's level.

Iceland has achieved higher average GDP growth than other Nordics but with substantially greater volatility. Over 2000–2023, Iceland's average annual GDP growth was 3.0% with a standard deviation of 4.2 percentage points, compared to Norway's 1.6% (std 1.5%), Denmark's 1.4% (std 2.2%), and Sweden's 2.0% (std 2.4%). The range spans from -8.3% in 2009 to +8.7% in 2007, reflecting boom-bust dynamics absent in the eurozone Nordics.

citations.report_title: 390.000 hektarar af landbúnaðarlandi breytast í skóg og votlendi

claims.claim_text: Hagkerfi Danmerkur hefur vaxið og lífskjör aukist á sama tíma og losun gróðurhúsalofttegunda dróst saman um tæp 45%.
